utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good morning, Pablo! How are you doing today?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Hi Rachel, I'm doing well, thank you. It's been a long week but I'm happy it's almost Friday.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Almost there! So tell me, how was your week at work? Anything interesting happening at the firm?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Actually we are working on a very big project for a client in the automotive sector.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So you're currently working on a big automotive project. That sounds intense.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, currently, sorry. We have been working on it for about three weeks now. The client wants us to help them with their digital transformation strategy.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Digital transformation - that's a big buzzword these days. What does it actually involve for this particular client?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Well, they want to automate many of their processes in the factory. And also they want to use AI for, how do you say... prediccion... predictive analytics, right?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Predictive analytics, exactly. So they're looking at automation and data-driven decision making?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, exactly. And it's very challenging because the managers there are not very used to working with technology. They are a traditional company.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That must create some interesting dynamics. Have you had to travel to their offices?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, I was in Barcelona on Monday and Tuesday for workshops with the stakeholders. We had to make a meeting with the regional directors and then explain the KPIs and the deliverables to the whole team.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a packed schedule. How did the workshops go?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: They went well, but I had to assist to many meetings that were not very productive. Some of them were just people repeating the same points.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So you had to attend a lot of meetings. I know that feeling - unproductive meetings are the worst.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Attend, yes. I always mix that one up. In Spanish we say asistir a una reunion.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It's one of those classic false friends. 'Asistir' looks like 'assist' but means 'attend' in English. You'll get it eventually - it just takes practice.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, I know, I know. It's very frustrating because I make the same mistake every time.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Don't be too hard on yourself. So apart from the travel, did you have any time in Madrid this week?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, Wednesday and Thursday I was in the Madrid office. I had to do a presentation for the partners about the project progress.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: You gave a presentation - how did that go?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It went quite well actually. I mean, in fact. The partners were happy with the results so far. They said the client is very satisfied.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good catch on 'actually' versus 'in fact'! And congratulations on the positive feedback. That must feel good.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you. Yes, it's nice when they recognize the work. We have been putting a lot of hours into this project.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I can imagine. Do you have any trips planned for next week?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: No, next week I will be working from home most of the days. Which brings me to something I wanted to talk about today.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Oh? What's that?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Remote work. I mean, the whole debate about working from home versus working in the office. It's a big topic in my company right now.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a great topic. And it ties in nicely with what I had planned for today - we're going to talk about the future of work. But first, let's start with your personal experience. What's the current policy at your firm?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: We have a hybrid model. We have to go to the office three days a week and we can work from home two days. But many people think is not enough flexibility.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Many people think it's not enough flexibility. Remember to include the subject 'it' in those embedded clauses.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Right. Many people think it is not enough flexibility. In Spain, the culture is very much about being present in the office. The managers want to see you there.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Interesting. So there's a strong presenteeism culture. Is that specific to consulting or is it a broader Spanish work culture thing?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think it's both. Consulting is very demanding in general, everywhere. But in Spain there is also this idea that if you leave early from the office, people think you are not working hard enough.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Even if you've completed all your work for the day?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, exactly. It's about perception. In the UK, from what I've heard, people leave at five or six and nobody judges them. But in Spain, if you leave at six, your colleagues look at you like you are lazy.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a fair observation about British work culture, though I'd say it varies by industry. How do you personally feel about the hybrid model?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I am agree with the hybrid model in principle. I think it gives a good balance.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: You agree with it - no 'am' needed there. Just 'I agree with the hybrid model.'

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Ah yes, I agree. In Spanish we say estoy de acuerdo, which is like I am in agreement. So I always add the 'am'.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly, it's a direct translation from the Spanish structure. In English, 'agree' is just a verb - 'I agree', 'you agree'. No 'to be' needed.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I agree with you. There, better?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect! So you agree with hybrid work. Tell me more - what are the advantages you see?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Well, the main advantage is flexibility. When I work from home, I can organize my time better. I don't waste time commuting, which in Madrid can be terrible.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: How long is your commute typically?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: About forty-five minutes each way if the traffic is normal. But sometimes it can be more than one hour. So when I work from home, I save almost two hours every day.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's significant. And what do you do with that extra time?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I usually go to the gym in the morning, or I can start working earlier and finish earlier. It depends of the day and what I have scheduled.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It depends on the day. In English, we say 'depend on', not 'depend of'.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Depend on. Yes, in Spanish it's depender de, so I translate the de as of. But it's on in English.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. Prepositions are tricky because they rarely translate directly between languages. So flexibility and saving commute time are two big advantages. What else?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I also think I'm more productive at home for certain types of work. When I need to do research or write reports, I concentrate better without the noise of the office.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That makes sense. Deep work requires fewer interruptions. What about the disadvantages though?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Well, the biggest problem is communication with the team. When you are in the office, you can just walk to someone's desk and ask a question. When you are remote, you have to send a message and wait for the answer.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: The spontaneous interaction element. Do you find that tools like Slack or Teams help bridge that gap?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: To some extent, yes. But it's not the same. Moreover, there is the problem of, how to say it, the informal conversations that happen in the coffee area. Those are important for building relationships with colleagues.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good point. Those watercooler conversations, as we call them, can be really valuable for team bonding and even generating new ideas.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Watercooler conversations, I like that expression. Yes, exactly. And in consulting, relationships are everything. You need to trust your team because you work under a lot of pressure.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Absolutely. So how does your team handle this? Do you have any strategies to maintain that team connection when people are remote?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: We try to make all the important meetings on the days when everyone is in the office. So Monday, Tuesday, and Thursday are our office days. When everyone is there the meeting is being very productive because we can discuss things face to face and we schedule the brainstorming sessions for those days too.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That sounds like a sensible approach. And what about Wednesday and Friday - those are your remote days?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes. Those are for focused work. We try to keep them free of meetings, although it's not always possible. Sometimes the client wants to have a call and you can't say no.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Right. Client demands take priority. Let me ask you something - do you think the hybrid model affects younger employees differently than senior ones?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a very interesting question. I think for junior consultants, it's harder to work from home because they need more guidance and mentoring. When I started in consulting, I learned a lot just by sitting next to experienced people and observing how they work.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That informal learning through observation - you can't really replicate that on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: No, you can't. And also, the junior people sometimes feel isolated when they work from home. They don't have the confidence to call a senior manager just to ask a small question. In the office, it's more natural.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a really insightful observation. What about the other side - are there senior people who resist remote work?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh yes. Some of the partners at my firm are very traditional. They think that if you are not in the office, you are not working. It's a matter of control, I think.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: A trust issue, essentially. Do you think that's generational, or is it more about management style?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think is a mix of both. The older partners grew up in a culture where being in the office was the only option. So for them, it's hard to imagine a different way of working.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I think it is a mix of both. Don't forget that 'it' - the subject is needed in English.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It is a mix of both, yes. But I also think the younger managers are more open to flexibility. They understand that productivity is not about hours in the office but about results.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a nice distinction - measuring output rather than input. Do you think the pandemic permanently changed attitudes?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Definitely. Before the pandemic, remote work was almost impossible in consulting. The culture was very much about being present, traveling to the client, being in the office late. Now, even the most traditional firms offer some flexibility.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Would you say it was a paradigm shift?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, absolutely. The pandemic demonstrated that people can be productive from home. And now you can't go back. If a company tries to force everyone back to the office full-time, people will leave. Especially the best talent.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a strong claim. Do you have evidence for that at your firm?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, we lost several good consultants last year because a competitor offered full remote work. For young people especially, flexibility is more important than a higher salary.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Interesting. So flexibility is becoming a competitive advantage in talent acquisition.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Exactly. And it's not just about remote work. It's about flexible hours, the possibility to work from different locations, sabbaticals. The new generation wants a different relationship with work.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Let me play devil's advocate for a moment. Isn't there a risk that fully remote teams lose their sense of company culture and identity?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Of course. I think that's why the hybrid model is the best solution. The meeting yesterday about this topic was being very long but we reached a good conclusion. You get the benefits of both worlds - the flexibility of remote work and the in-person connection.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: But isn't that a generalization? Different roles might need different arrangements.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's true. For example, the people in the creative department probably need more in-person collaboration. On the other hand, the data analysts can work perfectly from home. On the other hand, the managers need to be available for their teams.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I noticed you used 'on the other hand' twice. The second time, you might want a different connector. Maybe 'meanwhile' or 'at the same time' or even 'however'. 'On the other hand' is specifically used to present a contrasting point to the previous one.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, you're right. I tend to overuse that one. At the same time, the managers need to be available. Is that better?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Much better. Varying your discourse markers makes your speech sound more natural and sophisticated. Now, what about productivity metrics? How does your firm measure whether remote work is actually working?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: We use several KPIs. The main ones are client satisfaction scores, project completion rates, and billable hours. So far, the results show that productivity has not decreased with the hybrid model.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Has it increased?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: In some areas, yes. For example, the number of reports produced per week has increased because people have more focused time at home. But the results were very sensible to how you measure them.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: The results were very sensitive to how you measure them. 'Sensible' in English means 'reasonable' or 'practical'. The word you want is 'sensitive' - meaning they change depending on the measurement approach.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Sensitive, yes. Another false friend. In Spanish, sensible means sensitive. I will never learn these.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: You will! The fact that you recognize it is already progress. So the data is sensitive to measurement methodology. That's actually a very sophisticated observation.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you. I mean, you can make the numbers say whatever you want. If you only look at billable hours, remote work looks great. But if you look at client feedback, sometimes there are complaints about response times.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a nuanced view. You're saying the metrics tell different stories depending on which ones you choose to highlight.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Exactly. And the management uses the metrics that support their position. If they want to bring people back to the office, they show the client feedback data. If they want to keep the hybrid model, they show the productivity data.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a cynical but probably accurate reading of how data is used in organizations. Let's shift the discussion a bit. What about the impact on work-life balance?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: This is interesting because it has two sides. When you work from home, in theory you have more time for your personal life. But in practice, many people work more hours because the boundary between work and personal time disappears.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: The blurring of boundaries. Is that something you experience personally?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Sometimes, yes. When I'm at home, I might check my email at ten at night because my laptop is right there on the table. In the office, when I leave, I leave. At home, the office is always with me.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Have you set any boundaries for yourself?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I try to. I close my laptop at seven and I don't open it again. But it's difficult when you receive a message from a client at eight and you know it's urgent.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's the consulting life, I suppose. Do you think companies have a responsibility to protect employees' personal time?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Absolutely. In Spain, we have a law about the right to digital disconnection. Companies are supposed to respect employees' rest time and not send messages outside of working hours.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: And does it actually work in practice?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Not really. Everyone ignores it. The law exists but nobody enforces it. Especially in consulting, where the culture is to be always available.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It sounds like there's a gap between legislation and reality. Let me ask about another aspect - do you think remote work affects career progression?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a very good question. I think there is a risk of what they call proximity bias. The people who are in the office more often get more visibility, more opportunities. They are the ones who the partners see every day.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So there's an advantage to being physically present when it comes to promotions and assignments.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes. And this creates an unfair situation. For example, a mother who works from home more days because she has children may be overlooked for a promotion compared to a colleague who is in the office every day.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's an important equity concern. The flexibility that's supposed to help working parents could actually disadvantage them career-wise.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Exactly. It's a paradox. And it's something that companies need to be very careful about. They need to evaluate people based on their results, not on their physical presence.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I completely agree. Now let me ask you a hypothetical. If you could design the ideal work arrangement, what would it look like?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: If I would work from home... If I could choose, I would have two days in the office and three days remote. The office days would be for meetings, collaboration, and social interaction. The remote days would be for focused work.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good self-correction on the conditional. 'If I could choose' is the right form for a hypothetical. 'If I would' is a common error - we use past simple after 'if' in second conditionals.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, if I could choose. And I would also like to have the option to work from different cities sometimes. For example, go to Barcelona for a week and work from there.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: The digital nomad concept. Is that realistic in consulting though?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: For some roles, yes. If you don't have client meetings that require physical presence, you can work from anywhere with a good internet connection. But for client-facing roles like mine, it's more complicated.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: What about time zones? If your team is in Madrid and you're working from, say, Bali?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a problem, yes. The time difference can make collaboration very difficult. I think you need to be in a similar time zone at least, maybe plus or minus two hours.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a practical constraint. Let's also consider the environmental angle. Does remote work have environmental benefits?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Definitely. Less commuting means less carbon emissions. Less energy used in office buildings. It's better for the environment. But on the other hand, people use more energy at home for heating and electricity.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good use of 'on the other hand' there - you're presenting a genuine contrast. And what do the studies say about the net effect?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I have read that the net effect is positive for the environment, but it depend on many factors. Sorry, it depends on many factors. Like the distance of the commute and the type of office building.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good self-correction. 'It depends' - third person singular needs that 's'. So overall, you're cautiously in favor of hybrid work?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, I would say so. I think the key is flexibility. Not a one-size-fits-all approach, but a model that adapts to the needs of different teams and individuals.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a very balanced view. Now, you mentioned something interesting earlier about trust. Do you think remote work fundamentally changes the relationship between managers and employees?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think it forces managers to trust their employees more. When you can't see what someone is doing every minute, you have to trust that they are doing their work. And I think that's healthy.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Trust-based management versus surveillance-based management.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Exactly. And the companies that try to use surveillance tools, like tracking software on employees' computers, I think they are making a big mistake. It destroys trust and motivation.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Strong opinion. Do you know of companies that do that?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, some technology companies use software that takes screenshots of your computer every few minutes to make sure you are working. I think is terrible.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I think it is terrible - remember the 'it'. And I agree, that level of surveillance is counterproductive. It treats adults like children.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It is terrible, yes. If you don't trust your employees, the problem is not remote work. The problem is your hiring process or your management style.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Well said. That's a really sharp insight. Let's talk about something related - the impact of remote work on innovation. Some people argue that innovation requires in-person interaction.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think there is some truth to that. The best ideas often come from informal conversations, from brainstorming sessions where people are in the same room. It's hard to replicate that energy on a video call. If I would have to choose between Zoom brainstorming and in-person, I'd pick in-person every time.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Can you give me a specific example from your work?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes. Last month, we were working on a strategy for a client and we were stuck. We couldn't find the right approach. Then we had a meeting in the office, with whiteboards and post-its, and in two hours we had a completely new framework. I don't think that would have happened on Zoom.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a compelling example. The physicality of the whiteboard, the energy of the room. There's something about being co-located that sparks creativity.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes. But I also want to say that not all innovation requires that. Sometimes the best ideas come when you are alone, thinking deeply about a problem. So I think it's a combination.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Individual reflection and collaborative ideation - both are part of the innovation process.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Exactly. And a good hybrid model should allow for both. Time alone for deep thinking and time together for collaboration.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Let me throw another question at you. Do you think remote work has different implications for different cultures?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Absolutely. I was discussing about this topic with a colleague from the Nordic countries recently. In Scandinavia, remote work was already quite common before the pandemic. The culture is more individualistic and people are trusted to manage their own time.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I noticed you said 'discussing about this topic'. In English, we say 'discuss a topic' directly - no 'about' needed after 'discuss'.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, really? I was discussing this topic. In Spanish we say discutir sobre un tema. So the about feels natural.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Right, it's another case where the Spanish preposition doesn't transfer. 'Discuss something' - the verb takes a direct object. You can say 'talk about' or 'have a discussion about', but 'discuss' goes directly.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Got it. So, as I was saying, in Scandinavian countries the transition to remote work was much smoother than in southern European countries like Spain or Italy, where the culture is more relationship-oriented and people value face-to-face interaction.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a really interesting cultural comparison. Do you think Spain will eventually catch up?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think it's changing, but slowly. The younger generation is pushing for more flexibility. And the companies that don't adapt will lose talent. So it's an economic pressure more than a cultural change.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Market forces driving cultural change. That's a sophisticated argument. What about the role of technology in enabling remote work? Has your firm invested in better tools?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, we invested a lot during the pandemic. We now use Microsoft Teams for communication, Miro for virtual whiteboards, and we have a new project management tool that helps us track progress across remote teams.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Do those tools adequately replace in-person collaboration?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: They help, but they don't fully replace it. The technology is getting better, though. I think in a few years, with virtual reality and better video conferencing, the gap will be smaller.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Speaking of which, have you tried any VR collaboration tools?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: We did a pilot with Meta's Horizon Workrooms. It was interesting but still quite clunky. The headsets are uncomfortable for long meetings and the avatars look weird. I think it's not ready yet.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Early days for that technology, perhaps. Let me challenge you on something you said earlier. You mentioned that the best talent will leave if companies don't offer flexibility. But isn't that an oversimplification? Some people actively prefer working in the office.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: You're right, it is a generalization. Some people, especially those who live alone or have small apartments, prefer the office because it gives them a social space and a separation between work and home.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: And what about people who are early in their careers?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, I mentioned that before. Junior employees need the office more for learning and networking. So the preference for remote work is not universal. It depend on... it depends on your personal situation and your career stage.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Another good self-correction. You're catching those now. Alright, let's take a slight turn. Let's look at some of the language from our discussion so far. I noticed several collocation errors that I want to address.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay, I'm ready for the corrections.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Right. So let's talk about this. During our conversation, you said you needed to 'make a meeting' at one point. What do you think the correct collocation is?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Hold a meeting? Or have a meeting?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Both of those work beautifully. 'Hold a meeting', 'have a meeting', or 'schedule a meeting' if you're talking about setting one up. But not 'make a meeting'. What about when you talked about your Barcelona trip - you said you needed to 'do a presentation'?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Give a presentation?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly! Give a presentation, or deliver a presentation in more formal contexts. In Spanish, you 'hacer una presentacion', but in English, we give or deliver them.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Give a presentation, deliver a presentation. Okay. And what about 'take a decision'? I think I said that earlier too.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good memory! In English, we 'make a decision'. Though interestingly, in British English, 'take a decision' is sometimes used, but 'make a decision' is much more standard and natural.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: In Spanish we say tomar una decision, which is closer to take. That's why I use take.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. These collocations are particularly tricky because the Spanish verb-noun combination feels very logical when translated literally. Let me give you a few more important ones.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Please, yes.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Okay. 'Meet a deadline'. Not 'complete a deadline' or 'comply with a deadline'. We meet them.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Meet a deadline. In Spanish we say cumplir con un plazo. So I would probably say comply with.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Right. 'Comply with' is used for rules and regulations, but for deadlines, we meet them. And one more - 'do research', not 'make research'.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Do research. That one I think I know because I've seen it in academic papers. Do research, conduct research.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect. 'Conduct research' is the more formal version. Now, let me give you a little exercise. I'm going to say some wrong collocations and you tell me the correct ones. Ready?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Ready.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Make a mistake.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That one is correct! Make a mistake.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good! You caught me. How about 'do a complaint'?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Make a complaint.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent! 'Pay a visit'?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Pay a visit. That's correct. But in Spanish we say hacer una visita, so the temptation would be to say make a visit.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly! And 'take an exam'?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Take an exam. That's correct too. Or sit an exam in British English?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Very good! 'Sit an exam' is indeed the British variant. You know more than you think. How about 'assist to a conference'?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Attend a conference! You're testing me on the false friends now.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Of course I am! You're getting them right though. Let's try one more - 'raise a question'?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Raise a question. That's correct. Or ask a question. Both work, no?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Both work, yes. 'Raise a question' is more formal - it means to bring up a topic. 'Ask a question' is when you directly want an answer. Slightly different nuances.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I see. Raise is more about introducing the topic and ask is more about getting information.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. You've got a really good understanding of these distinctions. Now let me give you a summary of the key collocations to remember.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes please. I should write these down.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good idea. So: attend a meeting, not assist. Give or deliver a presentation, not do. Make a decision, not take. Meet a deadline, not comply with. Do or conduct research, not make. Have or hold a meeting, not make.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay, I'm taking notes. Attend, give, make, meet, do, have. Got it.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Great. The trick with collocations is that you just have to learn them as fixed pairs. There's no rule that explains why we 'make' a decision but 'do' research. You just have to memorize them.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It's frustrating but I understand. It's the same in Spanish. We have collocations that don't make logical sense either.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. Every language has them. Now, I want to come back to another pattern I noticed. You used some connectors in a way that wasn't quite right. You used 'moreover' at one point - do you remember what you said?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think I said something like 'moreover, there is the problem of informal conversations'. Was that wrong?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: 'Moreover' is used to add a stronger or more important point to something you've already said. It means 'in addition, and more importantly'. So it works when you're building up your argument. But in that context, you were just adding another point of equal importance. 'Also' or 'in addition' would have been more appropriate.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I see. So moreover is for when the second point is stronger than the first?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Right. Think of it as an escalation. 'The project is behind schedule. Moreover, we've exceeded the budget.' The budget issue is presented as even more serious.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a good example. I've been using moreover too much in my business emails. My boss is very comprehensive about my English mistakes but I want to improve.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Your boss is very understanding. 'Comprehensive' means 'complete' or 'covering everything' - like a comprehensive report. The word for someone who understands and is tolerant is 'understanding'.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Understanding! Yes. In Spanish, comprensivo means understanding. Another false friend.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: They're everywhere, aren't they? Let's make a quick list of the false friends we've covered today. Actually versus currently. Assist versus attend. Sensible versus sensitive. Comprehensive versus understanding. Am I missing any?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think those are the main ones. These are the ones I struggle with the most.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I'd recommend keeping a false friends journal. Every time you catch yourself using one, write it down with the correct English word and a sample sentence. Over time, the correct version will become automatic.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a good idea. I'll start doing that. Actually, I mean, in fact, I already have a vocabulary notebook but I don't have a specific section for false friends.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Ha! You caught yourself on 'actually' that time. Well done. And yes, a dedicated section for false friends would be very useful.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I'm getting better at catching them. It's just that they come out automatically, especially when I'm speaking fast.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's completely normal. They're fossilized errors - they've been reinforced so many times that they've become automatic. Breaking those patterns takes conscious effort and time.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Fossilized, I like that word. Like dinosaur fossils. My errors are ancient.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Ha! Well, we're going to excavate them and replace them with the correct forms. Now, let's move on to our next topic. I want to have a debate about AI and its impact on jobs. Are you ready?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, this is a topic I'm very interested on. I mean, interested in. In Spanish it's interesado en, so I guess the preposition is the same this time.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good catch! 'Interested in' is correct. And yes, this time the Spanish preposition 'en' does translate to 'in'. See, sometimes they do match!

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's almost worse. It makes it unpredictable.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Fair point! Alright, so let's set up the debate. I want you to take a position and defend it. Here's the proposition: AI will eliminate more jobs than it creates in the next decade. Do you agree or disagree?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a hard question. Let me think. I think I would argue that AI will create more jobs than it eliminates, but the transition period will be very painful for many workers.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Interesting position. So you're an optimist with caveats. Make your case.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Well, historically, every technological revolution has created more jobs than it has destroyed. The industrial revolution eliminated many manual jobs but created entire new industries. The internet destroyed some businesses but created millions of new ones. I think AI will follow the same pattern. We need to make research on this and look at the data carefully.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Do research, not 'make research'. But that's a solid historical argument. Now, isn't this time different? Previous revolutions replaced physical labor. AI replaces cognitive labor.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a good point. But I think even with cognitive tasks, AI will augment human capabilities rather than replace them entirely. For example, in consulting, AI can analyze data much faster than a human. But the interpretation, the strategy, the client relationship - those still require human judgment.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: But what about professions where the cognitive task IS the core of the job? Translators, for example, or data entry clerks, or even some aspects of legal work.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: You're right that those jobs are at risk. Translation is a good example because machine translation has become very good. But even there, human translators are still needed for nuanced, creative, or sensitive texts. The machine can do the basic translation but the human adds quality and cultural understanding.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Okay, I'll give you that. But what about the scale of job losses? Even if new jobs are created, they might require completely different skills. A factory worker who loses their job can't simply become an AI engineer.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's the transition problem I mentioned. The new jobs will require different skills, and retraining workers takes time and resources. I recommend that every government invests more in education and training. Companies also have a responsibility.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Do you think they will? Based on historical precedent, governments haven't been great at managing these transitions.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think the pressure will be so big that they will have to. If millions of people lose their jobs and there is no support for them, there will be social unrest. Governments will be forced to act.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a reactive approach though. Wouldn't it be better to be proactive?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Of course, but politics is often reactive. Look at climate change. Everyone knows it's a problem but we only act when there's a crisis. I think AI job displacement will be similar.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: A rather pessimistic view of governance for an AI optimist! Let me push you further. What about the argument that AI could lead to much greater inequality? The benefits go to the companies that own the AI while the costs are borne by workers.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a valid concern. We already see this with technology companies. The big tech firms have enormous profits and relatively few employees compared to traditional companies. If AI makes this worse, we could see a concentration of wealth that is dangerous for society.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So how do we address that? Some people have proposed a universal basic income. What do you think?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think universal basic income is an interesting idea but I'm not sure it's the right solution. Work is not just about money. It gives people purpose, identity, social connections. If we just give people money and they don't have work, I think many would suffer psychologically.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a very thoughtful perspective. The psychological dimension of work. Do you think new types of meaningful work will emerge?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I believe so. I think we'll see more jobs in areas that require emotional intelligence, creativity, and human connection. Healthcare, education, the arts, social work. These are areas where AI can help but cannot replace the human element.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Let me challenge that too. AI is already composing music, generating art, writing stories. What makes you think creativity is safe from automation?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's true, AI can generate creative content. But I think there's a difference between generating and creating. AI can produce a painting that looks like a Picasso, but it doesn't understand why Picasso painted the way he did. The meaning behind the art is human.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Interesting distinction. But does the audience care about the artist's intention, or just the experience of the art itself?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think they do care. When people buy art or listen to music, they connect with the artist's story, their emotion, their vision. AI doesn't have a story. It's just, how do you say... algorithms.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: For now, at least. What about your own industry? How is AI changing consulting?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It's already changing a lot. We use AI for data analysis, market research, and even for writing first drafts of reports. But the strategic thinking, the client conversations, the ability to understand a company's culture and politics - that's still very human.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So you see AI as a tool that makes consultants more efficient rather than replacing them?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, exactly. AI handles the routine tasks so we can focus on the higher-value activities. It's like when calculators were introduced. Accountants didn't disappear. They just started doing more complex work.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good analogy. Now I want you to argue the other side. Tell me why AI might actually eliminate more jobs than it creates.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, you want me to change sides? Okay, let me think. Well, the speed of AI development is much faster than previous technologies. The industrial revolution took decades to transform the economy. AI is transforming it in years.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Go on.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: And the scope is much broader. Previous technologies affected specific industries. AI affects almost every industry simultaneously. From healthcare to finance to manufacturing to services. The disruption is happening everywhere at the same time.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Now you're making a strong case for the pessimistic side. What else?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: The cost of AI is also important. When a technology is very expensive, adoption is slow and workers have time to adapt. But AI is becoming cheaper and cheaper. Any small company can use AI tools for almost no cost. This means the adoption will be very fast and the job losses will happen quickly.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent argument. And what about the quality of new jobs that might be created?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's another concern. The new jobs might be lower quality. We already see this with the gig economy. Many of the new jobs created by technology are precarious, without benefits or stability. AI might accelerate this trend.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So even if the number of jobs remains the same, the quality might decline. Good point. Now, I want to bring in some hedging language here because this is a topic where you need to express uncertainty.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Hedging language? Like what?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: When you're discussing predictions about the future, it's important to signal that you're not stating facts but expressing opinions or possibilities. Instead of saying 'AI will eliminate jobs', you could say 'AI is likely to eliminate jobs' or 'AI may well eliminate jobs' or 'There's a strong possibility that AI will eliminate jobs'.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I see. So it's about showing that you're aware of the uncertainty.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. And it makes your arguments sound more sophisticated and credible. Absolute statements like 'AI will destroy all jobs' sound simplistic. Hedged statements like 'AI could potentially displace a significant number of workers' sound more nuanced.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's very useful for my work presentations too. My clients prefer when we present scenarios with different probabilities rather than absolute predictions.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Precisely. Let me give you some useful hedging phrases. 'It's conceivable that', 'Evidence suggests that', 'It remains to be seen whether', 'One could argue that'. Can you use one in a sentence about AI?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Evidence suggests that AI will transform the labor market significantly, but it remains to be seen whether the net effect will be positive or negative.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a beautiful sentence. Well constructed, appropriately hedged, and it shows intellectual sophistication. Let's continue the debate with this language.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay. I think one could argue that the biggest risk is not the total number of jobs but the distribution. AI could create enormous wealth for a small group of people while leaving the majority behind.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent use of 'one could argue'. Keep going.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: And it's conceivable that this concentration of power could undermine democracy itself. If a few tech companies control the AI that runs the economy, they have enormous political influence.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: You're on fire with the hedging language. Now, what about the argument that AI could actually make work more human?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: How do you mean?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Well, if AI takes over the boring, repetitive tasks, humans could focus on the aspects of work that are more fulfilling - creativity, problem-solving, interpersonal relationships.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's the optimistic view. And I hope it's true. But it requires that companies use AI to complement human work, not to replace it. And the incentive for companies is to reduce costs, which usually means reducing headcount.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good use of 'complement' there. And you raise an important point about incentives. Companies are driven by profit, not by creating meaningful work.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Exactly. So without regulation, the natural tendency will be to use AI to replace workers whenever possible. That's why I think government intervention is necessary.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: What kind of intervention? Tax incentives? Regulations? Training programs?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: All of the above. I think governments should invest heavily in education, especially in digital skills. I suggest that every company contributes a percentage of their profits to retraining programs. They should also consider taxing automation to slow down job displacement.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: A robot tax, essentially. That's been proposed by several economists and even Bill Gates. Do you think it's practical?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It's controversial but I think is worth considering. The problem is that is very difficult to define what counts as automation. If a company uses AI to make one worker more productive and fires another worker, is that automation?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I heard two missing 'it's in there. 'I think it is worth considering' and 'the problem is that it is very difficult'. Those embedded clauses need their subjects.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: You're right. It is worth considering. It is very difficult to define. I keep forgetting the it in those structures.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It's one of the most common patterns I see with Spanish speakers. In Spanish, the subject can be dropped because the verb conjugation tells you who's doing the action. In English, we always need an explicit subject.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, it's the null subject thing. My English teacher in university used to tell me the same thing.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: And here you are, still working on it. But that's okay - some patterns take a very long time to fully correct. The important thing is awareness. Now, let's continue with the debate. What about the ethical dimension?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: The ethical dimension is very important. There are questions about bias in AI systems, about privacy, about accountability. If an AI makes a decision that affects someone's life, like denying them a loan or a job, who is responsible?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a crucial question. And how would you answer it?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think the company that deploys the AI should be responsible. They chose to use it, they should ensure it's fair and transparent. But the problem is that many AI systems are black boxes. Even the engineers don't fully understand how they make decisions.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: The explainability problem. It's one of the biggest challenges in AI ethics.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes. And in the European Union, there is a new regulation about AI. The AI Act. It requires that high-risk AI systems are transparent and explainable. I think it's a step in the right direction.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Have you had to deal with the AI Act in your consulting work?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, actually. I mean, in fact. We have several clients who are trying to understand what the regulation means for them and how to comply with it. It's becoming a significant area of work for consulting firms.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good catch on the 'actually' again! So AI regulation is itself creating new consulting jobs. That's ironic in the context of our debate.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Ha, that's true. AI creates jobs by needing people to regulate it and explain the regulations to companies.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Let me ask you about a specific scenario. Your firm is helping a bank implement an AI system for loan approvals. The AI is more efficient than human loan officers, but it shows bias against certain demographic groups. What do you recommend?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a scenario we have faced in real life. The recommendation would be to use the AI as a tool to assist the human loan officers, not to replace them. The AI can do the initial screening, but a human should review every decision, especially in cases where the AI recommends rejection.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: A human-in-the-loop approach. But doesn't that reduce the efficiency gains that made AI attractive in the first place?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, it does. But I think fairness is more important than efficiency in this case. A biased decision can destroy someone's life. You can't optimize for speed and ignore the ethical consequences.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Well argued. You're showing good ability to balance different considerations. Let me ask you one more question on this topic. I wish I will have... no, wait. Let me ask this differently. Do you think AI will eventually be more ethical than humans in these decisions?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I was about to make a mistake there too. I wish I could... no, I wish I had more confidence in AI being ethical. But I think it's possible that in the future, AI could be more consistent and fair than humans, because humans have unconscious biases too.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good attempt at the wish structure. It's 'I wish I had' for present wishes and 'I wish I could' for ability wishes. Not 'I wish I will have' - that mixes the wish structure with the future tense. The wish already implies wanting something different.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: So, I wish I had more time to study this topic. I wish I could spend a whole day researching it. Like that?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect examples. The past tense after 'wish' signals that the situation is hypothetical or contrary to reality.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: In Spanish we use the subjunctive for the same thing. Ojala tuviera mas tiempo. The structure is similar actually.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Interesting parallel. Now let's shift into the next phase of our lesson. I want you to think about a practical situation. You want to ask your boss for more flexible working hours. You're going to write a formal email requesting this. Shall we try?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, that's very relevant because I've been thinking about asking for exactly that. My current schedule doesn't allow me to pick up my son from school.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect, so this is real for you. Let's make it practical then. Before we start writing, let me ask - who is the recipient? What's your relationship like?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It's my direct manager, Alejandro. We have a good relationship but he's quite formal in written communication. He's a partner at the firm.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good. So we need a formal register but not overly distant since you have a good relationship. Let's start. How would you begin the email?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Dear Alejandro, I am writing to you to ask about the possibility of changing my working schedule.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good start. But 'ask about the possibility of changing' is a bit wordy. In formal business English, we might say 'I am writing to request a modification to my current working schedule.' It's more direct and professional.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I am writing to request a modification to my current working schedule. That does sound more professional.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good. Now, what's the next part? You need to explain why.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: As you may know, my son has recently started school and the school finishes at three thirty. Actually, currently, I am unable to pick him up because my working hours end at six.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good self-correction on 'actually' to 'currently'. But let's refine the tone. Instead of 'I am unable to pick him up', which sounds slightly negative, you could frame it as: 'Due to a recent change in my family circumstances, I would like to propose an adjustment to my schedule.' This frames it positively - you're proposing, not complaining.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I see. So it's about framing. Instead of presenting a problem, I present a solution.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. In business communication, always lead with the solution or the request, not the problem. What schedule change would you propose?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I would like to start at seven thirty instead of nine, and finish at three thirty instead of six. That way, I complete the same number of hours but I can pick up my son from school.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good. How would you phrase that in the email?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I would like to propose starting my working day at seven thirty and finishing at three thirty, maintaining the same total number of working hours. This adjustment would allow me to meet my family commitments while ensuring that my professional responsibilities are fully covered.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent! That's really well-phrased. You've maintained formal register, you've shown consideration for the business needs, and you've been specific about the proposal. Now, what else should you include?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Maybe I should mention that it won't affect my availability for client meetings? And that I can be flexible on days when there are important meetings in the afternoon?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect thinking. Anticipating objections is a great strategy in business writing. How would you phrase that?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I want to assure you that this change would not impact my availability for client meetings or team commitments. On days when afternoon meetings are scheduled, I would be happy to adjust my hours accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Brilliant. 'I want to assure you' is good, but 'I would like to assure you' is slightly more formal and appropriate for a written request. Also, 'I would be happy to adjust' is perfect - it shows flexibility and willingness.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I would like to assure you. Right, because we're writing, not speaking, so we should be more formal.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. The conditional 'would like' creates professional distance. In speech, 'I want to' is fine, but in formal writing, 'I would like to' is preferred. Now, how do you close the email?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I would be grateful if you could consider my request. I am available to discuss this matter at your convenience. Thank you for your time and understanding.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Very good closing. 'At your convenience' is appropriately deferential. One thing I'd add - you might want to suggest a trial period. This makes it easier for your boss to say yes because it doesn't feel permanent.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, that's a great idea. I could add: 'I would like to suggest a trial period of one month to assess the impact of this arrangement on my work and the team.'

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect. That shows maturity and pragmatism. You're making it easy for him to agree because there's an exit clause.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I learned that in consulting. Always give the client an easy way to say yes.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good business instinct. Now, what sign-off would you use?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Kind regards? Or Best regards?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Both work. 'Kind regards' is slightly warmer, which is appropriate since you have a good relationship. 'Best regards' is more neutral. Either is fine for this context.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I'll go with Kind regards since we have a good relationship.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good choice. Now, let me point out a few things about formal email writing in general. You did very well with register here. But there are some common mistakes that Spanish speakers make in formal emails.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Like what?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: One is using too many exclamation marks. In Spanish business emails, it's more common to use them. In English, exclamation marks in formal emails can seem unprofessional or overly emotional.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Really? I use exclamation marks a lot in my emails. I thought they were friendly.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: In informal emails, they're fine. But in a formal request like this one, stick to periods. The tone should come from your word choice, not punctuation.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a good tip. What else?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Another one is starting sentences with 'I hope this email finds you well.' It's become a cliche. If you have a good relationship with the person, just get to the point. 'Dear Alejandro, I am writing to...' is more efficient and professional.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: But I always start my emails with 'I hope this email finds you well'! Everyone in Spain does that.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It's not wrong, it's just overused. In British business culture, people tend to value brevity. Getting straight to the purpose shows respect for the reader's time.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I see. In Spanish business culture, we spend more time on pleasantries. It's considered polite to ask how someone is doing before getting to the business.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a genuine cultural difference. Neither is wrong, but when you're writing in English, especially to British or American recipients, lean towards brevity.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay. No more 'I hope this email finds you well'. Straight to business.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Well, you can still use it occasionally, especially with new contacts. Just don't make it your default opening for every email. Now, one more point about register. You managed to stay consistently formal in your email, which is excellent. Sometimes I see students mix formal and informal language in the same email. For example, starting with 'Dear Mr. Smith' and then writing 'Hey, just wanted to check in about the project'. That inconsistency is jarring.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I've seen that in emails from my colleagues. They start formal and then they get relaxed halfway through. It does look strange.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. Consistency of register is key. Choose your level of formality at the start and maintain it throughout. Now, let me ask you to do one more thing. Can you read back the complete email as you would send it?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay. Dear Alejandro, I am writing to request a modification to my current working schedule. Basically, my kid just started school and I need to sort out the pickup situation. Due to a recent change in my family circumstances, I would like to propose an adjustment.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Okay, hold on. Notice what happened there? You started with a formal register - 'I am writing to request' - and then dropped into very informal language - 'Basically, my kid just started school and I need to sort out the pickup situation.' That's a register inconsistency. Keep it formal throughout.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, you're right. I switched without even noticing. Let me try again. Due to a recent change in my family circumstances, I would like to propose an adjustment that would allow me to better balance my professional and personal commitments.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Specifically, I would like to propose starting my working day at seven thirty and finishing at three thirty, maintaining the same total number of working hours. I would like to assure you that this change would not impact my availability for client meetings or team commitments. On days when afternoon meetings are scheduled, I would be happy to adjust my hours accordingly.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent. Continue.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I would like to suggest a trial period of one month to assess the impact of this arrangement on my work and the team. I would be grateful if you could consider my request. I am available to discuss this matter at your convenience. Thank you for your time and understanding. Kind regards, Pablo.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That is a very well-written formal email. The register is consistent, the tone is professional but warm, you've anticipated objections, and you've included a trial period. I would send that with confidence.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you! I actually feel more confident about sending it now. I mean, I genuinely feel more confident. Was that an 'actually' mistake?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: No! That time 'actually' was correct! You used it to mean 'genuinely' or 'in reality', which is the correct English meaning. When you say 'I actually feel more confident', it means 'I truly feel more confident' - that's perfectly right.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh good! So I'm using the false friends correctly sometimes without even knowing it.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's progress! The trick is knowing WHEN 'actually' means 'in fact' versus when you're accidentally translating 'actualmente'. In that case, you genuinely meant 'in reality I feel confident', which is the correct usage.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think I understand the difference now. When I mean 'at this moment in time', I should use currently. When I mean 'surprisingly' or 'in reality', I can use actually.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect summary. Now, let me mention one thing about the email that we could improve. When you said 'due to a recent change in my family circumstances', that's deliberately vague. Is that intentional?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, I thought it was more professional not to go into too much detail. Is that wrong?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It depends on the relationship. Since you have a good relationship with Alejandro, you might want to be slightly more specific. Something like 'As my son has recently started school with a three-thirty finish time'. This makes the request more relatable and harder to refuse.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a good point. It's a balance between being professional and being human.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. In consulting, you probably over-index on formality sometimes. But a personal touch can make your communication more persuasive.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I'll add that detail then. Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Great. Now, let's think about what might happen after you send this email. What if Alejandro says no?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Then I would need to negotiate. Maybe propose alternative arrangements. For example, maybe I could start earlier just two days a week instead of every day.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good fallback position. In negotiation, always have a Plan B. What language would you use to respond to a rejection?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I would say something like 'Thank you for considering my request. I understand your concerns. Would it be possible to discuss alternative arrangements that might work for both of us?'

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent. You're not pushing back aggressively. You're acknowledging their position and opening a dialogue. Very diplomatic.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Years of consulting have taught me the art of diplomacy. You never take a direct confrontation with the client... I mean with the boss.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Ha! The boss as client - that's actually a useful way to think about it. Alright, let's talk about one more aspect of the email. What if Alejandro responds positively but with conditions?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Then I would accept the conditions and confirm in writing. Something like 'Thank you for your approval. I would like to confirm the arrangement as discussed: I will begin working from seven thirty starting next Monday, with a review after one month. Please let me know if you require anything further.'

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Professional and clear. You're confirming the details to avoid misunderstandings. That's good practice. Notice how you used 'I will begin' - future simple for a definite plan. And 'please let me know if you require' - formal and polite. Your register is spot on.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you. I feel like this is one of the most practical exercises we've done. I'm going to use this email for real.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's the best outcome for a lesson - when the practice directly applies to your real life. Do send it and let me know how it goes in our next session!

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I will. Now, you mentioned the AI debate earlier. Can we continue that a bit more? I have some thoughts I want to practice expressing.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Of course. We still have time. What thoughts?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I've been thinking about what AI means for education. My son is five years old and I wonder what the world will look like when he enters the job market. What skills should he learn?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a really pertinent question. What's your instinct?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think the skills that will be most valuable are the ones that AI cannot replicate. Critical thinking, creativity, emotional intelligence, the ability to work with people from different cultures and backgrounds.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: The so-called soft skills, which are actually the hardest skills to develop.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Exactly! I hate the term soft skills because it makes them sound less important. They should be called essential skills.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: I couldn't agree more. Some people have started using the term 'power skills' instead. Do you think the education system is preparing children for this future?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Not at all. The education system, at least in Spain, is still based on memorization and standardized tests. It doesn't teach children how to think critically or solve problems creatively. It's still a nineteenth-century model.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a strong criticism. Is it fair though? There have been educational reforms.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Some reforms, yes. But the fundamental model hasn't changed. Children still sit in rows, listen to a teacher, take notes, and then take exams. The world has changed dramatically but schools look the same as fifty years ago.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: What would you change if you could redesign the education system?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I would focus on project-based learning, where children work in teams to solve real problems. I would teach them how to learn, not what to learn. Because the knowledge they need will change, but the ability to learn new things will always be valuable.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Learning how to learn - metacognition. That's a powerful concept. What role would technology play in your ideal school?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Technology should be a tool, not the center. Children should learn to use AI and other technologies, but they should also learn to think without them. If you become too dependent of technology, you lose the ability to think independently.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Dependent on technology, not 'of'. Another preposition!

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Dependent on. In Spanish it's dependiente de. The de keeps tripping me up.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Spanish 'de' maps to several English prepositions depending on the context. 'Depend on', 'dependent on', but 'independent of'. English is wonderfully inconsistent.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Wonderfully inconsistent indeed. So my son should learn to be independent of technology but also able to use it effectively.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect use of 'independent of'! You immediately applied the correction. Now, I want to push you on something. You said AI can't replicate critical thinking. But can't it? AI can analyze arguments, identify logical fallacies, evaluate evidence.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: You're right, AI can do those things in a technical sense. But critical thinking also involves questioning your own assumptions, understanding the social context of a problem, considering ethical implications. I don't think AI can truly do that.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Yet. It can't do that yet.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Fair point. Maybe I'm being too optimistic about human uniqueness. But I hope that there will always be something that distinguishes human thinking from artificial intelligence.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's a philosophical question more than a technical one. And it's okay not to have a definitive answer. Now, let me ask you about one more aspect. How do you think AI will affect language learning?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, that's very relevant for us! I think AI translation tools will make language learning less necessary for basic communication. But for deeper cultural understanding and professional fluency, human language skills will still be essential.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Do you think students will still take language lessons with human tutors in ten years?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I think so, yes. AI can help you practice vocabulary and grammar, but it can't give you the kind of feedback that you give me. It can't understand my frustration with false friends or know exactly when to push me harder. There's a human element in teaching that AI can't replace.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Well, thank you for the vote of confidence! I hope you're right. And I think the fact that you're here, investing in your English with a human tutor, shows that you value that human connection.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Absolutely. And honestly, I've learned more about English from our conversations than from any app or AI tool. The context, the humor, the cultural references - you can't get that from a machine.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's very kind. Let me bring us back to the language now. In this last part of the debate, you used hedging language well. But I want to point out something about your conditional structures.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: What about them?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Earlier you said 'If I would work from home' before correcting yourself. This is a very common error for Spanish speakers. In second conditional sentences - hypothetical present or future - we use past simple after 'if', not 'would'. Can you give me an example?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: If I worked from home every day, I would be more productive. Is that right?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
