utterances.text: Perfect. 'If I worked' - past simple, 'I would be' - would plus infinitive. The 'would' goes in the result clause, never in the 'if' clause.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: If AI replaced all consultants, I would need to find a new career. Another one?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent. Now give me a third conditional - a hypothetical about the past.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: If I had studied computer science instead of business, I would have been better prepared for the AI revolution.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Textbook perfect. 'If I had studied' - past perfect, 'I would have been' - would have plus past participle. Excellent control of that structure.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you. The third conditional I can usually get right because the structure is very clear. It's the second conditional where I make mistakes because in Spanish, we can use the conditional in both parts of the sentence.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's an excellent observation about the source of the error. Understanding why you make a mistake is the first step to correcting it. Alright, shall we move to our wrap-up?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Sure. But can I ask one more question about the debate topic?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Of course.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: What do you personally think? Will AI create or destroy more jobs?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Honestly, I think it will do both simultaneously. Some sectors will see massive job losses while entirely new industries emerge. The net effect will depend on how well societies manage the transition. I lean cautiously optimistic, but I think the transition period will be brutal for many workers.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I agree. It's not a simple yes or no question. The answer consist in... consists of many nuances.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good self-correction. 'Consists of' is right. You started with 'consist in' which is the Spanish structure - consistir en. In English it's 'consist of'.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Consists of. And there's also 'consist of' versus 'comprise', right? Or 'is comprised of'?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good question. 'Comprise' is tricky. Strictly speaking, 'the team comprises five people' - no 'of' needed. But 'is comprised of' is so widely used that it's generally accepted, even though prescriptivists will tell you it's wrong. For professional writing, I'd recommend 'consists of' or 'is made up of' - they're safe and clear.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I'll stick with consists of then. Safer.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good strategy. Now, let's do our proper wrap-up. I want to review the key points from today's lesson. We covered a lot of ground.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, it was a very full lesson.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So let's go through what we worked on. First, we had a great discussion about remote and hybrid work, where you showed very strong argumentative skills. Your ability to present balanced views has really improved.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you. I enjoy debating these topics. They are relevant to my work and my life.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's what makes them effective for language learning. Now, in terms of errors, let's review. What were the main categories?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: False friends, definitely. Actually versus currently, assist versus attend, sensible versus sensitive, comprehensive versus understanding.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good memory. And what else?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: The collocations. Attend a meeting, give a presentation, make a decision, meet a deadline, do research.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent recall. And what about grammar patterns?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: The missing it in embedded clauses. I think it is important, not I think is important. And the prepositions - depend on, interested in, consist of.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: And the conditionals?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: If I worked, not if I would work. The would goes in the result clause.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect summary. You clearly understand the rules. The challenge is applying them in spontaneous speech, which takes practice. And what about the new skills we worked on?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: The hedging language - evidence suggests, it remains to be seen, one could argue. And the formal email writing - register, structure, tone.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Excellent. Now let me give you my assessment. Your overall fluency is very strong for a B2 level. You communicate complex ideas effectively, you argue persuasively, and you use sophisticated vocabulary.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: But?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: But there are fossilized errors that keep coming back. The false friends, the null subjects, the prepositions. These are the things that prevent you from sounding like a C1 speaker. The good news is that you're increasingly catching them yourself, which means you're developing self-monitoring skills.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I notice them more when I'm speaking slowly and carefully. When I speak fast, especially in stressful situations like client meetings, I revert to the old patterns.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's completely normal. Under cognitive load, we fall back on our most established patterns. The goal is to make the correct forms your established patterns through repeated practice.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: How can I practice outside of our lessons? Besides the false friends journal you mentioned.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Great question. Here are my suggestions. First, when you write emails at work, check for the false friends before sending. Create a checklist. Second, try recording yourself speaking about a topic for five minutes and then listen back for errors.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Recording myself? That sounds painful.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It is at first, but it's incredibly effective. When you listen to yourself, you hear things that you don't notice while speaking. Third, read The Economist or the Financial Times. The business language will help you with collocations and formal register.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I already read The Economist sometimes. But I should do it more regularly.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Make it a daily habit, even if it's just one article. Pay attention to how they use collocations and discourse markers. That kind of input helps build your intuition for natural English.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I'll try. What about podcasts? Any recommendations?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: For your level and interests, I'd recommend the HBR IdeaCast - it's Harvard Business Review's podcast. Business-focused, well-articulated English, and relevant to your work. Also try Freakonomics Radio for more general intellectual content.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I know Freakonomics. I listened to it in Spanish. I should switch to the English version.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Definitely. Since you already know the content in Spanish, you'll follow the English version more easily. That's actually a great learning strategy.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I'll start tomorrow. On my commute to the office.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect. Forty-five minutes of English podcast each way. That's an hour and a half of exposure every day. Your listening skills will skyrocket.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: If I don't fall asleep on the metro.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Ha! Choose engaging topics and you'll stay awake. Now, for homework. I want you to do two things before our next lesson.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay, tell me.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: First, revise the email we worked on today. Write the final version and send it to me before our next lesson. I'll give you feedback.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: The email to Alejandro about flexible hours?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Yes. Write it out properly, check for all the false friends and collocations we discussed, and make sure the register is consistent throughout.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay, I'll do that this weekend.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good. Second, I want you to prepare a three-minute argument about AI and jobs. Pick a specific position and defend it using the hedging language we practiced. Record yourself and listen back. Note any errors you catch.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Three minutes. Okay. And should I write it first or just speak spontaneously?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Speak spontaneously. Don't write a script. The goal is to practice fluency and self-monitoring in real time. You can make bullet points with your main arguments, but don't write out full sentences.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's harder but I understand why. In real conversations, I don't have a script.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. And that's when the fossilized errors come out. So this is targeted practice for exactly the situations where you need it.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Okay, I'll do it. What topic specifically? The same one we debated today?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Yes. 'AI will create more jobs than it eliminates.' Argue for or against. Use at least three hedging phrases and try to include some of the collocations we practiced.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Got it. Evidence suggests, it remains to be seen, one could argue. And attend meetings, make decisions, do research.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect. You remember them all. Now, is there anything else you want to discuss before we finish?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Just one thing. I have a presentation next week for a client about digital transformation. It will be in English because the client is a British company. Any tips?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Ah, that's important. A few quick tips for presenting in English to British clients.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Please.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: First, understatement works better than overselling with British audiences. Instead of 'This is an incredible opportunity', try 'This presents a rather interesting opportunity'. British culture values restraint.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's very different from how we present in Spain. In Spanish business culture, enthusiasm is valued. We tend to be very passionate and expressive. I am not sure if I can change my style that much, to be honest.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Exactly. You don't need to suppress your personality, but dial it down slightly. Be enthusiastic but measured.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Enthusiastic but measured. I like that. What else?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Second, structure your presentation with a clear beginning, middle, and end. British audiences appreciate signposting: 'I'd like to start by', 'Moving on to', 'In conclusion'. It helps them follow your argument.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I already do that but I can be more explicit about it. Sometimes I jump between points without signposting.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Third, and this is specific to you - watch out for the false friends. In a high-pressure situation like a client presentation, those automatic errors are most likely to appear. Maybe write 'attend not assist' and 'currently not actually' on a card and keep it in front of you.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's a practical tip. I'll put it on a post-it on my laptop screen.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good idea. And one more thing - if you're not sure of a word, it's perfectly acceptable to pause and say 'What I mean is...' or 'In other words...' and rephrase. It sounds more natural than using the wrong word.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Better to pause than to make an error?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: In a professional context, yes. A brief pause to find the right word shows care and precision. Using the wrong word shows carelessness, even if it's just an L1 interference issue.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That makes sense. I'll practice the presentation this weekend too.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good. If you want to send me your slides, I can check the English on them before the presentation.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Oh, that would be very helpful. I'll send them on Sunday.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect. I'll review them and send you feedback on Monday. That gives you time to incorporate any changes before the presentation.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you, Rachel. That's really kind of you.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: It's part of the service! Now, let me give you a quick overall assessment for today.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Go ahead, I'm ready for the verdict.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Your strengths today: excellent argumentative skills, good use of business vocabulary, strong ability to self-correct when prompted. Your fluency is very natural and you communicate complex ideas effectively.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: And the areas for improvement?

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: The fossilized L1 transfer errors: false friends, null subjects, preposition choices. These are your biggest barriers to C1. The good news is that you caught several of them yourself today, which shows growing metalinguistic awareness.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Metalinguistic awareness. That's a nice way to say I know I make mistakes but I still make them.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Ha! Well, awareness is the first step. Production takes longer. But you're making real progress, Pablo. Compared to where you were six months ago, you're catching errors faster and your formal writing has improved significantly.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's nice to hear. Sometimes it feels like I'm not improving at all because the same errors keep coming back.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: That's the plateau effect. At B2, progress feels slower because the errors are more deeply ingrained. But trust me, you are improving. The frequency of those errors has decreased, and your ability to self-correct has increased.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you. That's encouraging. I'll keep working on it.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: And I'll keep pushing you. That's what I'm here for.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: I appreciate it. Even when it's frustrating, I know the corrections help.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: So to summarize the homework: one, revise and finalize the email to Alejandro. Two, record a three-minute argument about AI and jobs using hedging language. Three, start a false friends section in your vocabulary notebook. And optionally, send me your presentation slides.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Got it. Email, recording, false friends journal, and slides. I'll be busy this weekend.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Not too busy, I hope. Rest is important too! When is your presentation, by the way?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: It's on Wednesday afternoon. So I have a few days to prepare.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good. And our next lesson is next Friday, right?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Yes, same time. Ten o'clock.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect. In our next lesson, I'd like to do some more work on conditionals and maybe explore reported speech, which is another area where Spanish speakers often have difficulties.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Reported speech, yes. I know I have problems with the tense changes. He said that he was going, he said that he had been... it gets confusing.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: We'll work on it. It's all about backshifting - moving the tense one step into the past. We'll practice with some real scenarios from your work.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That would be great. I often have to report what clients said in meetings, so it's very practical.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Perfect. We'll use that context. Is there anything else before we finish?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: No, I think we covered everything. Thank you for a great lesson, Rachel. I always enjoy our sessions.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Thank you, Pablo. You're a pleasure to teach. You bring real engagement and curiosity to every lesson, which makes my job much easier.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: That's very kind. And I promise to do all the homework this time.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: This time? Have you been skipping homework?

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Well, last week I didn't do the writing exercise because I had a very busy week. But I read the articles you recommended.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Reading is good. But the writing is where you consolidate what you've learned. It forces you to think about accuracy in a way that speaking doesn't.

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: You're right. I'll make it a priority this time. I'll do the email first because it's the most useful for my real life.

utterances.speaker: Rachel
utterances.text: Good strategy. Prioritize what has the most immediate practical value. Alright, Pablo, have a wonderful weekend and good luck with the presentation next week!

utterances.speaker: Pablo
utterances.text: Thank you, Rachel. Have a great weekend too. And I'll send you the slides on Sunday.
